role_hints.role: Specialized designers (fashion, jewelry, textile, footwear, costume, float/exhibit)
role_hints.oews_basis: role_mix is 4-digit 5414 (interior 18.4% + graphic 15.4% top the cell — sibling contamination); 27-1022 Fashion Designers and other specialty design SOCs sit in the unlisted tail. Hypothesis for the 6-digit code: est. ~35-45% of wage bill
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~35-45% (hypothesis — record is 4-digit and misses the core occupations)
role_hints.note: Sketching, technical packs/spec sheets, colorways, pattern variants and CAD iterations are heavily AI-exposed (generative fashion/jewelry design tools exist now); physical sampling, fit sessions, materials sourcing and trend/taste judgment persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 12.4% at 4-digit; est. ~12-15% here
role_hints.note: In founder-branded studios the owner is the brand and the client relationship; ops automate.
Brand/licensing relationships with apparel houses, manufacturers and retailers persist; lookbook and pitch production automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 3.5% at 4-digit + production coordinators in the tail
role_hints.note: Tech-pack handoff, vendor follow-up and sample tracking automate substantially; factory-facing physical QC does not.

role_hints.oews_basis: 53-7062 Laborers & Material Movers 1.2% at 4-digit + sewers/sample-makers/model-makers in the tail, est. ~8-12%
role_hints.note: Physical sample construction, fittings, exhibit/float fabrication — not AI-replaceable.
Studio admin, invoicing, logistics paperwork — near-fully automatable.

source_hints.source: CFDA (Council of Fashion Designers of America) reports and industry studies
source_hints.why: The fashion-design professional body — closest source for practice structure and technology adoption in the likely-dominant sub-segment.

source_hints.source: McKinsey/Business of Fashion 'State of Fashion' reports (gen-AI in design chapters)
source_hints.why: Documented analysis of generative AI adoption in fashion design workflows — usable for current_adoption_pct in the dominant sub-segment.

source_hints.source: Fashion/design trade press (Business of Fashion, WWD) coverage of freelance design services pricing
source_hints.why: Evidence on how independent design services bill brands and manufacturers (per-collection fees, day rates, royalties).

source_hints.source: No dedicated owner-demographic source known for this code
source_hints.why: Flagging honestly: expect to fall back on Census/ABS owner-age data for the 5414 group or general small-business proxies.

source_hints.source: No known PE roll-up or consolidator set for specialized design services
source_hints.why: Verify before assuming any consolidation activity — acquisition activity in fashion is of brands (with product revenue), not design-services firms.

capture_questions: Specialized design services bill per collection, per piece, as day rates, or occasionally as royalties on produced goods — under each, when generative tools collapse design and tech-pack hours, does the fee hold (clients buy taste and brand access, not hours) or do manufacturers and brands re-scope to fewer paid design days? Identify which billing form dominates the dominant sub-segment.
Who owns the client relationship — the studio or the named designer? In fashion/jewelry design the designer's name and taste ARE the product; assess what an acquirer retains when the founder leaves.

What stops apparel brands and manufacturers from generating designs in-house with AI tools plus their existing technical teams — is external design demand structurally shrinking the way industrial design's did via insourcing?

Does any segment of this code carry durable non-design revenue (physical sample rooms, exhibit/float fabrication, licensing royalties) that survives AI, and how large is it relative to pure design fees?
terminal_value_question: This is a catch-all of unlicensed, taste-based design services where the deliverable (sketches, specs, patterns, renderings) is exactly what generative AI produces — no statute, stamp or physical gate protects the fee, except in the fabrication-heavy niches (exhibits, floats, sample-making) where the product is a physical object. Does paid external demand survive as anything beyond named-designer brand cachet and physical fabrication — i.e., is AI cutting these operators' costs or generating their entire product? Answer per sub-segment, not for the code as a whole.
special_notes: Catch-all code: identify the top-3 revenue sub-segments, score the DOMINANT one, and set heterogeneous: true. Hypothesis (flagged as such): fashion design services is the dominant sub-segment by revenue (NYC/LA concentration), with jewelry, textile, footwear, costume and exhibit/float design as the tail.

Role-mix contamination: role_mix is 4-digit 5414 — interior (18.4%) and graphic (15.4%) designer shares belong to 541410/541430; the code's actual core occupations (27-1022 Fashion Designers et al.) are absent from the top-10. Rebuild and flag as hypothesis.

Fragmentation: 2,871 of 3,386 firms are under 5 employees; n_band 82 rests on the 20-99 class (57 firms). No documented consolidator set exists — an M score built on assumed exit demand is unsupported; verify or discount.

Key-person risk is maximal: the designer's name, taste and brand relationships are the firm; expect near-total value portability and structure any roll-up thesis around fabrication/licensing assets, not fee books.

Boundary confusion: designers employed by apparel brands are in the brand's code (315/424/etc.); interior design 541410, graphic design 541430, industrial/product design 541420; theatrical set design may book under arts codes (711). Fashion-industry data overwhelmingly describes brands, not independent design-services firms — do not import brand-market economics into this services code.
